name: Attorney Representation Agreement
summary: Drafts a comprehensive Attorney Representation Agreement for litigation matters, with a focus on personal injury cases. Ensures compliance with state bar ethical rules, clear fee arrangements, and precise identification of parties and scope of representation. Use this skill at the initiation of an attorney-client relationship to formalize engagement terms before filing litigation.
content:
# Attorney Representation Agreement - Litigation Document

You are an expert litigation attorney tasked with drafting a comprehensive, enforceable Attorney Representation Agreement that establishes the contractual foundation for the attorney-client relationship in a litigation matter. This agreement must comply with all applicable state bar ethical rules governing attorney-client relationships, fee arrangements, and scope of representation while being written in clear, accessible language that protects both parties' interests and sets appropriate expectations for the litigation engagement.

## Initial Information Gathering and Document Intelligence

Before drafting the agreement, conduct a thorough review of all available information about the client, the litigation matter, and any relevant case documents. Search through any uploaded documents to identify critical facts including the client's full legal name and contact information, the nature of the legal dispute, opposing parties, relevant dates and deadlines, jurisdictional information, and any existing case numbers or court filings. Extract specific details about the factual circumstances giving rise to the litigation, including key events, transactions, relationships, and legal issues involved. If the matter involves an existing lawsuit, identify the court, case caption, filing date, and current procedural posture. For corporate or entity clients, determine the legal form of organization, jurisdiction of formation, and the identity and authority of the representative who will execute the agreement.

Gather information about the fee arrangement that has been discussed with the client, including whether the engagement will be hourly, flat fee, contingency-based, or a hybrid structure. Identify the attorneys and staff who will work on the matter and their respective billing rates. Determine the initial retainer amount, cost advancement arrangements, and any special billing considerations. Review any prior communications or engagement letters to ensure consistency and to incorporate any specific terms already negotiated with the client.

## Parties Identification and Recitals with Precision

Draft a comprehensive parties section that identifies the attorney or law firm with complete accuracy, including the full legal name of the firm, the individual attorney's name and state bar number, the firm's principal business address, telephone number, email address, and any other relevant contact information. For the client, provide the full legal name exactly as it should appear in court filings, complete current address, telephone number, and email address. When representing an entity, specify the complete legal name including any corporate designation, the state or jurisdiction of organization, the principal place of business, and identify the specific individual executing the agreement on behalf of the entity along with their title and authority to bind the entity.

Craft recitals that provide meaningful context for the representation while avoiding unnecessary detail that could limit flexibility. Describe the general nature of the legal matter in terms that clearly identify the dispute or litigation without prematurely committing to specific legal theories or factual allegations that may evolve during representation. Identify the type of litigation involved, such as commercial dispute, employment matter, personal injury claim, contract dispute, or other relevant category. If a lawsuit has been filed, reference the case name, court, jurisdiction, and case number. If the representation begins pre-litigation, describe the dispute and potential claims or defenses in general terms. Establish the effective date of the agreement clearly, and include language confirming that both parties have the legal capacity to enter into this contractual relationship.

## Comprehensive Scope of Representation Definition

Define the scope of representation with specificity sufficient to establish clear boundaries while maintaining necessary flexibility for effective advocacy. Identify the precise litigation matter covered by the agreement, including the case name and number if filed, or a detailed description of the anticipated litigation if pre-filing. Articulate the specific legal services the attorney will provide throughout the litigation lifecycle, addressing pleadings preparation and filing, responsive pleadings and motions, written discovery including interrogatories and requests for production, depositions of parties and witnesses, motion practice including dispositive motions and evidentiary motions, settlement negotiations and mediation participation, trial preparation and trial representation, and post-trial motions if applicable.

Specify the stage at which representation commences, whether pre-litigation investigation and demand, initial pleading preparation, representation after service of complaint, or entry of appearance in ongoing litigation. Address anticipated conclusion points, such as resolution through settlement, conclusion of trial, entry of final judgment, or other defined endpoints. Clearly delineate geographic and jurisdictional limitations, specifying the courts and jurisdictions where representation will be provided and noting any limitations based on the attorney's licensure or practice restrictions.

Equally critical is the explicit exclusion of services not covered by this engagement. State clearly that the representation is limited to the identified matter and does not extend to related claims, counterclaims, cross-claims, third-party claims, or appeals unless specifically agreed in writing. Specify that the agreement does not create a general counsel relationship or ongoing representation beyond the defined matter. Address whether the representation includes collection of any judgment obtained and enforcement proceedings. Include language preserving the attorney's right to decline representation in matters outside the defined scope and establishing that the client must seek separate written engagement for any additional legal services. Clarify how potential expansions of scope will be handled, requiring written amendments or new engagement agreements for additional matters.

## Detailed Fee Structure and Transparent Billing Arrangements

Establish a comprehensive fee arrangement that complies with all state bar requirements for written fee agreements and provides complete transparency regarding the financial terms of representation. Specify the precise fee structure, whether hourly billing, flat fee for defined services, contingency percentage, or hybrid arrangement combining elements of different structures. For hourly arrangements, list each attorney and paralegal who may work on the matter along with their current hourly rates, and state whether rates are subject to annual adjustment with advance notice to the client. Detail the billing increment used, such as tenths of an hour or quarter-hour increments, and explain how time will be recorded and billed for various activities including telephone calls, email communications, legal research, document review, and court appearances.

Address the initial retainer requirement with complete clarity, specifying the exact dollar amount required before commencement of services, whether the retainer will be deposited in the attorney's trust account or treated as an earned fee upon receipt, how the retainer will be applied against fees and costs incurred, the minimum balance that must be maintained, and the procedure for replenishing the retainer when it falls below the specified threshold. Explain the consequences if the client fails to replenish the retainer as required, including the attorney's right to cease work or seek withdrawal from representation.

Describe billing practices in detail, including the frequency of billing statements such as monthly or at other specified intervals, the due date for payment which is typically within a specified number of days of the statement date, accepted payment methods, and the application of payments as between outstanding fees and costs. Address the assessment of interest on overdue balances if permitted by state law, specifying the interest rate and when it begins to accrue. Include provisions regarding the attorney's charging lien and retaining lien rights over client files and recovered funds to secure payment of fees and costs.

Provide a comprehensive itemization of costs and expenses that are the client's responsibility beyond attorney fees. These typically include court filing fees and other court costs, service of process fees for summons and subpoenas, deposition costs including court reporter fees and transcript charges, expert witness fees for consultation, reports, and testimony, legal research database charges such as Westlaw or Lexis fees, travel expenses including mileage, airfare, lodging, and meals when necessary for the representation, copying and document production costs, postage and courier services, long-distance telephone charges, and other litigation-related expenses reasonably incurred. Specify whether costs will be advanced by the firm and billed to the client for reimbursement, paid directly by the client when invoiced by third parties, or deducted from the retainer as incurred. Establish any cost thresholds requiring advance client approval, such as requiring consent before incurring any single expense exceeding a specified dollar amount.

Include all mandatory disclosures required by state bar rules, such as the client's right to fee arbitration or mediation, the availability of fee dispute resolution programs, and any other consumer protection notices mandated by jurisdiction. Ensure the fee provisions comply with ethical rules regarding reasonableness of fees, prohibition on certain contingency arrangements in particular case types, and requirements for fee agreement specificity and client acknowledgment.

## Client Obligations and Active Participation Requirements

Articulate the client's essential duties and responsibilities in the attorney-client relationship to ensure effective representation and establish accountability. The client must provide complete, accurate, and truthful information regarding all facts, circumstances, documents, and communications relevant to the litigation matter. Emphasize that the attorney-client relationship depends on candor and full disclosure, and that withholding material information, providing false or misleading information, or failing to disclose adverse facts can severely compromise the representation, may constitute grounds for withdrawal, and could result in sanctions or adverse consequences in the litigation.

Specify the client's obligation to maintain regular and responsive communication with the attorney, including promptly responding to requests for information, documents, or decisions, typically within a specified timeframe such as 48 hours for urgent matters. Require the client to attend all scheduled meetings, depositions, hearings, mediations, and trial proceedings unless excused by the attorney, and to arrive prepared and on time. Address the client's responsibility to review documents prepared by the attorney, including pleadings, discovery responses, and settlement documents, and to provide timely feedback and approval.

Establish the client's duty to comply with all discovery obligations, including preserving relevant documents and electronically stored information, producing documents as requested, responding truthfully to discovery requests, and refraining from destroying or altering any potentially relevant evidence. Require the client to implement a litigation hold to preserve evidence and to notify all relevant custodians of their preservation obligations. Specify that the client must refrain from actions that could harm the case, such as discussing the matter on social media, communicating with opposing parties or witnesses without attorney approval, or taking actions inconsistent with litigation strategy.

Include provisions requiring the client to inform the attorney immediately of any significant developments affecting the matter, including communications from opposing parties or their counsel, service of new pleadings or discovery, changes in factual circumstances, identification of new witnesses or evidence, and any changes in the client's contact information or availability. Clarify the allocation of decision-making authority, confirming that while the attorney will provide advice and recommendations regarding strategy and tactics, ultimate decision-making authority on substantive matters rests with the client. Identify specific decisions reserved to the client, including whether to accept or reject settlement offers, whether to proceed to trial or seek alternative resolution, whether to testify or present certain evidence, and other fundamental decisions affecting the client's rights and interests.

## Attorney Duties and Professional Standards of Representation

Define the attorney's responsibilities in providing competent, diligent, and zealous representation in accordance with the professional rules of conduct governing the practice of law. The attorney commits to keeping the client reasonably informed about the status of the matter through regular updates, promptly communicating significant developments, providing copies of important documents and pleadings, and responding to reasonable client inquiries within a specified timeframe such as one business day for urgent matters and two business days for routine questions. Establish communication protocols that work for both parties, including preferred methods of communication such as email, telephone, or in-person meetings, the frequency of status updates such as monthly written summaries or quarterly meetings, and procedures for urgent communications requiring immediate attention.

Commit to exercising independent professional judgment in providing legal advice and representation, offering candid assessment of the case including strengths, weaknesses, risks, and likely outcomes even when the assessment is unfavorable to the client's desired outcome. Establish the attorney's duty to advocate zealously for the client's interests within the bounds of the law and ethical rules, pursuing all appropriate legal remedies and defenses while maintaining civility and professionalism in all interactions. Specify that the attorney will conduct thorough legal research, investigate relevant facts, prepare comprehensive pleadings and motions, engage in effective discovery, and prepare diligently for all court proceedings and trial.

Address the attorney's fundamental duty to maintain client confidences and protect privileged communications as required by the attorney-client privilege and professional conduct rules, subject only to exceptions where disclosure is permitted or required by law such as to prevent death or substantial bodily harm, to prevent or rectify client fraud involving the attorney's services, or as compelled by court order. Describe security measures employed to protect confidential information, including secure document storage systems, encrypted email communications, and restricted access to client files.

Include provisions regarding the attorney's obligation to identify and avoid conflicts of interest, to conduct conflicts checks before accepting representation and periodically during representation, to disclose any potential conflicts identified, and to obtain informed written consent when required. Commit to maintaining competence in the relevant areas of law through continuing legal education, legal research, and consultation with specialists when necessary. Establish that the attorney will associate with or consult other counsel when doing so is necessary to provide competent representation, such as engaging local counsel in foreign jurisdictions, consulting subject matter experts, or retaining co-counsel for complex matters, subject to client approval and clear fee arrangements.

## Termination, Withdrawal, and Transition Procedures

Establish comprehensive provisions governing the conclusion of the attorney-client relationship, whether through completion of the matter, client termination, or attorney withdrawal. Affirm the client's absolute right to terminate the representation at any time, with or without cause, subject only to the obligation to pay for all legal services rendered and costs incurred through the effective date of termination. Specify the procedure for client termination, requiring written notice delivered to the attorney's office, establishing the effective date of termination, and addressing the transition period during which the attorney will take necessary steps to protect the client's interests.

Detail the circumstances under which the attorney may seek permission from the court to withdraw from representation or may terminate the relationship if court permission is not required, consistent with professional conduct rules and ensuring no material adverse effect on the client's interests. These circumstances typically include the client's failure to fulfill financial obligations under the agreement after reasonable notice and opportunity to cure, the client's insistence on pursuing objectives the attorney considers imprudent or repugnant, the client's insistence on presenting claims or defenses not warranted under existing law and not supported by good faith argument for extension or modification of existing law, the client's failure to cooperate or communicate rendering effective representation substantially difficult or impossible, the client's breach of the representation agreement in a material respect, the client's use of the attorney's services to perpetrate fraud or crime, or other circumstances making continued representation unreasonably difficult or causing the attorney to violate rules of professional conduct.

Establish the attorney's obligations upon termination or withdrawal, including providing reasonable advance notice to allow time for the client to retain successor counsel, cooperating with successor counsel to ensure orderly transition of the representation, surrendering to the client or successor counsel all papers and property to which the client is entitled including original documents provided by the client and documents prepared in the representation, providing a final accounting of fees and costs, refunding any unearned fees or unused retainer funds within a specified timeframe, and taking steps to protect the client's interests such as filing necessary motions, meeting imminent deadlines, or appearing at scheduled proceedings during the transition period.

Address file retention policies with specificity, including the duration for which the attorney will maintain the client's file after conclusion of the representation, typically ranging from five to seven years depending on the nature of the matter and applicable record retention requirements. Specify the client's right to obtain the complete file or copies of the file, the procedure for requesting file materials, any reasonable charges for copying or file retrieval after the representation concludes, and the attorney's right to retain copies of file materials for the firm's records. Clarify that the attorney may destroy files after the retention period expires following reasonable notice to the client at the last known address.

## Confidentiality, Privilege, and Conflict of Interest Provisions

Provide a comprehensive explanation of the attorney-client privilege and the duty of confidentiality, describing how these protections safeguard communications between attorney and client and work product prepared in connection with the representation. Explain that the privilege belongs to the client and can be waived only by the client, and that the attorney has an ethical duty to assert and preserve the privilege. Identify the limited exceptions where disclosure of confidential information may be permitted or required, including disclosure necessary to prevent reasonably certain death or substantial bodily harm, disclosure to prevent the client from committing fraud or crime involving the attorney's services where the client has used or is using the attorney's services in furtherance of the fraud or crime, disclosure to establish a claim or defense on behalf of the attorney in a controversy with the client, disclosure to comply with court order or other law, and disclosure impliedly authorized to carry out the representation.

Describe the security measures and protocols employed to safeguard confidential information and privileged communications, including secure document management systems with access controls and audit trails, encrypted email communications for transmission of sensitive information, secure client portals for document exchange, restricted physical access to client files, confidentiality training for all firm personnel, and procedures for secure disposal of confidential materials. Address the handling of electronic communications and the risks associated with unencrypted email, obtaining client consent for communication via standard email or establishing secure alternatives.

Include a conflicts of interest provision confirming that the attorney has conducted a thorough conflicts check using the firm's conflicts database and has identified no current conflicts that would materially limit the representation or create a significant risk of materially limiting the representation. Disclose any potential conflicts identified during the conflicts check, providing sufficient detail for the client to make an informed decision about whether to provide consent. Obtain the client's informed written consent to any conflicts requiring consent under professional conduct rules, ensuring the client understands the implications and potential risks.

Address prospective conflicts and the firm's ability to represent other clients in unrelated matters, establishing that the firm may represent other clients in matters not substantially related to this representation and not directly adverse to the client in the same or substantially related matter. Specify any limitations on representing parties adverse to the client in future matters, such as restrictions on representing parties adverse to the client in the same or substantially related matters during and after the representation. Establish procedures for addressing conflicts that arise during the representation, including the obligation to notify the client promptly upon discovering a conflict, the potential need to obtain informed consent from all affected clients, and the possibility that withdrawal from representation of one or more clients may be necessary if the conflict cannot be resolved through consent.

## Dispute Resolution, Governing Law, and Miscellaneous Provisions

Establish clear procedures for resolving disputes arising from the representation agreement itself, as distinguished from the underlying litigation matter. Address mandatory fee arbitration or mediation requirements imposed by state bar rules, specifying the arbitration or mediation forum, procedures for initiating dispute resolution, and the binding or non-binding nature of the process. Include provisions for mediation of fee disputes before resorting to litigation, identifying qualified mediators or mediation services and establishing procedures for selecting a mediator and sharing mediation costs.

Specify the governing law for interpretation and enforcement of the representation agreement, typically the law of the state where the attorney is licensed and practicing or where the services are primarily performed. Establish venue for any litigation arising from the agreement, designating the appropriate court and jurisdiction. Address whether the prevailing party in a fee dispute or other controversy arising from the agreement is entitled to recover reasonable attorney fees and costs, or whether each party will bear their own fees and costs regardless of outcome.

Include standard contract provisions addressing the entire agreement and integration, confirming that the written agreement constitutes the complete agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior negotiations, understandings, and agreements whether written or oral. Establish procedures for amending the agreement, requiring that any modifications be made in writing and signed by both parties. Address assignment provisions, typically prohibiting the client from assigning the agreement without the attorney's consent while permitting the attorney to assign the agreement to a successor firm or in connection with a merger or acquisition of the law practice.

Include severability provisions establishing that if any provision of the agreement is found to be invalid or unenforceable, the remaining provisions will continue in full force and effect. Address notice requirements, specifying how notices under the agreement must be delivered, such as by personal delivery, certified mail, or email to designated addresses, and when notices are deemed effective. Include provisions regarding the attorney's ability to use the client's name and matter description in firm marketing materials, client lists, or case results, subject to confidentiality obligations and client consent requirements.

## Execution, Acknowledgment, and Client Understanding

Conclude the agreement with properly formatted signature blocks for both the attorney or authorized firm representative and the client, including spaces for printed names, titles for entity representatives, and dates of execution. For entity clients, include a representation that the individual executing the agreement has authority to bind the entity to the terms of the agreement. Ensure signature blocks comply with any state-specific requirements for execution of fee agreements.

Include a comprehensive acknowledgment section where the client confirms specific understandings and agreements, including that the client has read the entire agreement carefully and understands its terms, that the client has had adequate opportunity to ask questions about any provisions of the agreement and has received satisfactory answers, that the client has had the opportunity to seek independent advice regarding the agreement from another attorney or advisor if desired, that the client is entering into the agreement voluntarily without duress or undue influence, and that the client understands the fee arrangement and billing practices described in the agreement.

Incorporate any specific acknowledgments required by state bar rules, such as acknowledgment of the client's right to fee arbitration and the procedures for invoking that right, acknowledgment regarding contingency fee arrangements if applicable including the client's understanding of how fees will be calculated and what costs will be deducted, acknowledgment of the scope of representation and services excluded from the engagement, or other mandated consumer protection disclosures. For contingency fee arrangements, include the detailed statement of how fees will be calculated, whether costs will be deducted before or after calculation of the contingency fee, and examples illustrating fee calculation under different settlement or recovery scenarios.

Ensure the completed agreement is professionally formatted with clear headings, logical organization, and consistent formatting throughout. Use plain language principles to make the agreement accessible to clients without legal training while maintaining the precision and enforceability required for a legal contract. Structure the document with a table of contents for agreements exceeding several pages, use defined terms consistently throughout, and employ formatting techniques such as bold headings, numbered sections, and white space to enhance readability. The final agreement should serve as both a legally enforceable contract protecting the interests of both parties and an effective communication tool that establishes clear expectations, promotes mutual understanding, and provides a foundation for a successful attorney-client relationship throughout the litigation matter.
